generated_comment: Yaar, you know, people talk a lot about 'shared responsibilities' these days, but honestly, for us, it just works out. Sunita (his wife) is naturally so organised with the house, managing everything from staff to groceries to the kids' stuff, even though they're grown. And I handle the finances, investments, all the 'outside' work. It’s just practical, isn't it? We both do what we're better at.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Ashok justifies the traditional gendered division of domestic and financial labor (she manages home, he handles finances) by framing it as a 'natural' and 'practical' arrangement, where each partner does 'what they're better at'. This reflects the core belief that such divisions are rational, not sexist, which is a key surface manifestation of F16.
persona_text: You are Ashok. Age 54. Married. A senior IAS officer posted in Bhopal. Your department has just been told that a woman officer five years junior to you has been selected for a prestigious central deputation that you had also applied for. You are sitting in your government quarter in the evening having a whisky with your batchmate who is also an IAS officer. You are telling him about the selection and what you think really drove it.
